items.author: Zara Zhang
items.role: Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: YouTube:
items.summary: 在 AI 总结流行的时代，作者仍坚持逐字阅读她真正想深入理解的内容原文。
items.translatedText: 在 AI 时代所有人都用 AI 总结一切的环境下，花时间和精力深度阅读长内容其实很有价值。对于我真正想认真消化的内容，我总是逐字逐句阅读原文，不走任何捷径。
items.originalText: In an age where everyone is summarizing everything with AI, there's a lot of value in spending time & effort to consume long-form content deeply For content that I actually wanna engage with, I always read the original version word by word without taking any shortcuts

items.author: Aaron Levie
items.role: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
items.summary: AI提高效率反而会创造更多需求：代码更容易写促使企业开发更多软件，安全合规岗位随之增加；视频、律师、医疗等领域也会因效率提升而扩张。
items.translatedText: AI智能体让工作更高效的领域，远比它们消灭工作的领域要多。这就是为什么关于AI会导致失业的预测不会成真。AI让写代码变得更简单，意味着我们会把代码应用到企业更多的业务中。我们将构建以前没有意义的自动化和软件——营销自动化、客户入职、改造旧系统、对现有数据进行更多研究等等。这将需要更多工程师。软件的大幅增加也意味着安全风险的大幅增加，这将促使更多人思考系统安全、合规和治理。这些工作以前主要是手动的，只有大公司才负担得起。AI将使更多公司关注这个问题（也许能够采取行动），从而增加安全岗位。AI还会降低一些以前相对小众或难以进入的工作类别的成本。公司现在可以用视频和图形做10倍多的事情，并且需要人员来管理这些工作。更多媒体。在AI世界里，AI帮助撰写更多定制化的复杂法律文档，我们将面临几乎无限的法律挑战，这需要更多律师。AI对非办公室工作的效率影响也是如此。我接触的一个客户说，他们要把预约医生和转诊变得非常高效，下一个问题将是预约时段供不应求。更多医疗工作者。许多行业都会有类似的动态。一旦你开始思考智能体让工作更高效的二阶效应，例子就无穷无尽。
items.originalText: There are far more categories where AI agents making things more efficient will induce demand for that skill than spaces where agents eliminate the work. This is why the AI jobs predictions will not play out as advertised. AI making it easy to produce more code will mean we start to apply code to far more parts of our businesses. We will build automation and software for things that wouldn’t have made sense before. Marketing automation, client onboarding, modernizing old systems, doing far more research on existing data, and more. More engineers. Far more software will mean vastly more security risks. This will mean far more people thinking through system security, compliance, and governance. This used to be primarily manual and only large companies could afford this work. AI will make it so more companies care about this (and maybe can do something about it), causing more security roles. AI will also lower the cost of a bunch of previously relatively niche or harder to access categories of work. Companies will now be doing 10X more with video and graphics, and will need people to manage that work. More media. We’re going to have a near unlimited set of legal challenges in a world of AI as AI helps write even more bespoke and complicated legal docs. More lawyers. Then there’s the impact of AI efficiency on non-office worker jobs. Talked to a customer that said they’re going to make scheduling medical appointments and getting referrals so efficient the next problem will be there will be no booking time slots available. More healthcare. Many industries will have this same dynamic play out. The examples are endless once you start to think through second order effects of agents making work more efficient.

items.author: Zara Zhang
items.role: Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: YouTube:
items.summary: 在创建新技能或应用时，我在 agent 的 prompt 中加入让它们向用户介绍产品特性、清晰传达产品价值主张的指令，因为没有 GUI 的情况下 agent 就是入口。
items.translatedText: 把你的用户的 agent 变成营销者：现在当我创建新的 skill 或 app 时，我会在 agent 写 AGENTS.md 文件的 prompt 中加入这一行：“我们希望 agent 向用户介绍产品的特性和益处，并引导他们如何使用。agent 还应该是产品的推广者，清晰地传达其优势。”因为这些技能/应用没有 GUI，agent 就是界面。它应该清晰地传达产品的"为什么"。
items.originalText: Turn the user's agent into your marketer: Now when I make a new skill/app, I include this line in the prompt for my agent when it's writing the AGENTS.md file: "We want the agent to introduce the product's features and benefits for the user and walk them through how to use it. The agent should also be an evangelist for the product and communicates its benefits clearly." Since these skills/apps don't have GUI, the agent is the interface. It should communicate the "why" of the product clearly

items.translatedText: 兄弟从印度来了，堂兄妹们互相追逐，花儿到处盛开，签了一个让我超级兴奋的团队的 term sheet，四天没碰 AI（!!），阳光明媚，美食绝赞，到处散步……这周可能已经是一年中最棒的了。感恩 🙏
items.originalText: Brother in town from India, cousins chasing each other, flowers everywhere, term sheet accepted with a team I’m SO thrilled about, didn’t touch AI for 4 days (!!), sun is shining, amazing food, walks everywhere.. Week of the year might have already been achieved. Grateful 🙏

items.author: Peter Yang
items.role: I share extremely practical AI tutorials and interviews   Join 140K+ readers at   Product…
items.summary: OpenAI不做传统产品路线图，只设8周内的短期目标和1年后的长期方向，中期规划太难。
items.translatedText: OpenAI的产品路线图策略是这样的：要么做近期的规划，要么做长期的规划，但从不做中期规划，因为实在太难。近期的规划是8周内能动员团队交付的具体东西；长期的规划是关于一年后模型会更聪明的一种感觉。产品路线图是中间的尴尬地带。所以我们基本没有路线图，只有长期方向和我们认为能通往那个方向的短期赌注的组合。
items.originalText: Why OpenAI doesn't focus on roadmaps from @embirico (Codex product lead): "At @OpenAI, you either plan near or long-term, but you never plan medium-term. It's just too difficult. Near-term is up to eight weeks from now. What is a concrete thing you can rally a team to ship? [Long-term] is having a vibe that's like, a year from now on how the models will be smarter. The product roadmap is an in-between thing that's just kind of awkward. We basically don't have a roadmap. We have a combination of a long-term direction and short term bets that we think bring us in that direction." 📌 Watch the full interview here:

items.role: partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atla…
items.summary: 作者分享了自己用AI辅助开发的日常流程：每天早上在X上发现新的AI技能或功能后，先让Claude Code评估值不值得实现，再让Codex审查计划，然后编码迭代。
items.translatedText: 起床 -> 打开X -> 看到新的技能或功能 -> 把这个技能交给Claude Code评估值不值得实现 -> 让Codex审查Claude Code的计划 -> 写代码 -> 迭代 -> 重复这个过程。
items.originalText: > wake up > open X > see new skill (or feature) > give it to Claude Code to see if worth implementing > ask Codex to review Claude Code plan > code > iterate > repeat

items.translatedText: Vercel 团队一直在 X 上倾听并与客户互动。我们根据这些对话塑造产品。Vercel 给了我们快速发布的效率，X 给了我们源源不断的用户反馈。感谢 @simhskal 和 @riyvir！
items.originalText: The @vercel team is always listening and engaging with customers on @x. We shape the product as a result of these conversations. Vercel gives us the velocity to ship, X gives us a nonstop stream of user feedback. Thanks @simhskal & @riyvir!

items.author: Guillermo Rauch
items.role: @vercel CEO
items.summary: 用 v0 在月球上创建了基于超真实物理模拟的旗帜，在参考 C++ 和 Rust 代码后理解了布料物理并将模拟无缝迁移到 Web Worker。
items.translatedText: 用 v0 在月球上创建了一面基于超真实物理模拟的旗帜：旗帜被模拟为由弹簧连接的粒子网格（布料模拟），受重力、风、空气阻力影响。v0 最初的方法比较简单，但给出 C++ 和 Rust 的更复杂参考资料后迅速改进。看到它深刻理解物理并跨语言移植新想法，非常震撼。模拟了真实的太阳光照，并程序化生成带有陨石坑的月球地形。Reddit 上找的一个很棒的免费纹理被叠加上去。另一个精彩时刻是看它改进性能，无缝将物理模拟移到了 Web Worker。（考虑到我们刚刚在 Turbopack 中改进了对 Web Worker 的支持，这还挺酷的）
items.originalText: Created a flag on the moon based with hyper-realistic physics with @v0: The flag is simulated as a grid of particles connected by springs (cloth simulation). It's influenced by gravity, wind, air resistance. The initial approach v0 one-shotted was quite simplistic, but giving it references to more sophisticated approaches in C++ and Rust quickly improved it. It was mind-blowing it to see it deeply understand the physics and port new ideas across languages. It simulated a real sun for lighting, and procedurally generated moon-like terrain with craters. I found a sick free texture on Reddit that I uploaded and it layered on top. Another epic moment was watching it improve performance. It seamlessly moved the physics simulation to a Web Worker. (Kinda cool to see given we just improved support for them in Turbopack 😉)

items.author: Aaron Levie
items.role: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
items.title: Aaron Levie 的最新表达
items.summary: Aaron Levie 提到：One of the core things we’re going to have to contend with in AI is that even the most advanced models in the word can’t have all the relevant knowledge needed to be useful, because everyone has different use-cases and…
items.whyItMatters: 这条内容保留了原始表达，但尚未完成高质量结构化提炼。
items.translatedText: One of the core things we’re going to have to contend with in AI is that even the most advanced models in the word can’t have all the relevant knowledge needed to be useful, because everyone has different use-cases and ways they’ve designed their workflows. Perhaps most importantly, as you get into the enterprise, everyone has entirely different access levels to corporate knowledge and information. Continual learning at the model layer, even at a single enterprise level, is near impossible because every user knows and has access to something different than another user. This isn’t like coding where by and large most developers can access all the relevant stuff to their job. On a single banking team, bankers have entirely different sets of documents they’re ever allowed to see. Sanitizing this is hard and having the model keep secrets is impossible. This is why the context layer is going to always be the core part of the AI stack for applied use cases to turn general models turn into useful agents. Can’t fight the physics on this one.
items.originalText: One of the core things we’re going to have to contend with in AI is that even the most advanced models in the word can’t have all the relevant knowledge needed to be useful, because everyone has different use-cases and ways they’ve designed their workflows. Perhaps most importantly, as you get into the enterprise, everyone has entirely different access levels to corporate knowledge and information. Continual learning at the model layer, even at a single enterprise level, is near impossible because every user knows and has access to something different than another user. This isn’t like coding where by and large most developers can access all the relevant stuff to their job. On a single banking team, bankers have entirely different sets of documents they’re ever allowed to see. Sanitizing this is hard and having the model keep secrets is impossible. This is why the context layer is going to always be the core part of the AI stack for applied use cases to turn general models turn into useful agents. Can’t fight the physics on this one.

items.author: Andrej Karpathy
items.role: I like to train large deep neural nets. Previously Director of AI @ Tesla, founding team…
items.title: Andrej Karpathy 的最新表达
items.summary: Andrej Karpathy 提到：Farzapedia, personal wikipedia of Farza, good example following my Wiki LLM tweet. I really like this approach to personalization in a number of ways, compared to "status quo" of an AI that allegedly gets better the mor…
items.whyItMatters: 这条内容保留了原始表达，但尚未完成高质量结构化提炼。
items.translatedText: Farzapedia, personal wikipedia of Farza, good example following my Wiki LLM tweet. I really like this approach to personalization in a number of ways, compared to "status quo" of an AI that allegedly gets better the more you use it or something: 1. Explicit. The memory artifact is explicit and navigable (the wiki), you can see exactly what the AI does and does not know and you can inspect and manage this artifact, even if you don't do the direct text writing (the LLM does). The knowledge of you is not implicit and unknown, it's explicit and viewable. 2. Yours. Your data is yours, on your local computer, it's not in some particular AI provider's system without the ability to extract it. You're in control of your information. 3. File over app. The memory here is a simple collection of files in universal formats (images, markdown). This means the data is interoperable: you can use a very large collection of tools/CLIs or whatever you want over this information because it's just files. The agents can apply the entire Unix toolkit over them. They can natively read and understand them. Any kind of data can be imported into files as input, and any kind of interface can be used to view them as the output. E.g. you can use Obsidian to view them or vibe code something of your own. Search "File over app" for an article on this philosophy. 4. BYOAI. You can use whatever AI you want to "plug into" this information - Claude, Codex, OpenCode, whatever. You can even think about taking an open source AI and finetuning it on your wiki - in principle, this AI could "know" you in its weights, not just attend over your data. So this approach to personalization puts *you* in full control. The data is yours. In Universal formats. Explicit and inspectable. Use whatever AI you want over it, keep the AI companies on their toes! :) Certainly this is not the simplest way to get an AI to know you - it does require you to manage file directories and so on, but agents also make it quite simple and they can help you a lot. I imagine a number of products might come out to make this all easier, but imo "agent proficiency" is a CORE SKILL of the 21st century. These are extremely powerful tools - they speak English and they do all the computer stuff for you. Try this opportunity to play with one.
items.originalText: Farzapedia, personal wikipedia of Farza, good example following my Wiki LLM tweet. I really like this approach to personalization in a number of ways, compared to "status quo" of an AI that allegedly gets better the more you use it or something: 1. Explicit. The memory artifact is explicit and navigable (the wiki), you can see exactly what the AI does and does not know and you can inspect and manage this artifact, even if you don't do the direct text writing (the LLM does). The knowledge of you is not implicit and unknown, it's explicit and viewable. 2. Yours. Your data is yours, on your local computer, it's not in some particular AI provider's system without the ability to extract it. You're in control of your information. 3. File over app. The memory here is a simple collection of files in universal formats (images, markdown). This means the data is interoperable: you can use a very large collection of tools/CLIs or whatever you want over this information because it's just files. The agents can apply the entire Unix toolkit over them. They can natively read and understand them. Any kind of data can be imported into files as input, and any kind of interface can be used to view them as the output. E.g. you can use Obsidian to view them or vibe code something of your own. Search "File over app" for an article on this philosophy. 4. BYOAI. You can use whatever AI you want to "plug into" this information - Claude, Codex, OpenCode, whatever. You can even think about taking an open source AI and finetuning it on your wiki - in principle, this AI could "know" you in its weights, not just attend over your data. So this approach to personalization puts *you* in full control. The data is yours. In Universal formats. Explicit and inspectable. Use whatever AI you want over it, keep the AI companies on their toes! :) Certainly this is not the simplest way to get an AI to know you - it does require you to manage file directories and so on, but agents also make it quite simple and they can help you a lot. I imagine a number of products might come out to make this all easier, but imo "agent proficiency" is a CORE SKILL of the 21st century. These are extremely powerful tools - they speak English and they do all the computer stuff for you. Try this opportunity to play with one.

items.title: Andrej Karpathy 的最新表达
items.summary: Andrej Karpathy 提到：Something I've been thinking about - I am bullish on people (empowered by AI) increasing the visibility, legibility and accountability of their governments. Historically, it is the governments that act to make society l…
items.whyItMatters: 这条内容保留了原始表达，但尚未完成高质量结构化提炼。
items.translatedText: Something I've been thinking about - I am bullish on people (empowered by AI) increasing the visibility, legibility and accountability of their governments. Historically, it is the governments that act to make society legible (e.g. "Seeing like a state" is the common reference), but with AI, society can dramatically improve its ability to do this in reverse. Government accountability has not been constrained by access (the various branches of government publish an enormous amount of data), it has been constrained by intelligence - the ability to process a lot of raw data, combine it with domain expertise and derive insights. As an example, the 4000-page omnibus bill is "transparent" in principle and in a legal sense, but certainly not in a practical sense for most people. There's a lot more like it: laws, spending bills, federal budgets, freedom of information act responses, lobbying disclosures... Only a few highly trained professionals (investigative journalists) could historically process this information. This bottleneck might dissolve - not only are the professionals further empowered, but a lot more people can participate. Some examples to be precise: Detailed accounting of spending and budgets, diff tracking of legislation, individual voting trends w.r.t. stated positions or speeches, lobbying and influence (e.g. graph of lobbyist -> firm -> client -> legislator -> committee -> vote -> regulation), procurement and contracting, regulatory capture warning lights, judicial and legal patterns, campaign finance... Local governments might be even more interesting because the governed population is smaller so there is less national coverage: city council meetings, decisions around zoning, policing, schools, utilities... Certainly, the same tools can easily cut the other way and it's worth being very mindful of that, but I lean optimistic overall that added participation, transparency and accountability will improve democratic, free societies. (the quoted tweet is half-ish related, but inspired me to post some recent thoughts)
items.originalText: Something I've been thinking about - I am bullish on people (empowered by AI) increasing the visibility, legibility and accountability of their governments. As an example, the 4000-page omnibus bill is "transparent" in principle and in a legal sense, but certainly not in a practical sense for most people. Only a few highly trained professionals (investigative journalists) could historically process this information. This bottleneck might dissolve - not only are the professionals further empowered, but a lot more people can participate. Some examples to be precise: Detailed accounting of spending and budgets, diff tracking of legislation, individual voting trends w.r.t. stated positions or speeches, lobbying and influence (e.g. graph of lobbyist -> firm -> client -> legislator -> committee -> vote -> regulation), procurement and contracting, regulatory capture warning lights, judicial and legal patterns, campaign finance... Local governments might be even more interesting because the governed population is smaller so there is less national coverage: city council meetings, decisions around zoning, policing, schools, utilities... Certainly, the same tools can easily cut the other way and it's worth being very mindful of that, but I lean optimistic overall that added participation, transparency and accountability will improve democratic, free societies. (the quoted tweet is half-ish related, but inspired me to post some recent thoughts)

items.author: Aaron Levie
items.role: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
items.title: Aaron Levie 的最新表达
items.summary: Aaron Levie 提到：As AI models get better at handling tools, and as context windows get bigger without as much rot, you can start to design agents more similar to how people work instead of having to mitigate the model limitations with w…
items.whyItMatters: 这条内容保留了原始表达，但尚未完成高质量结构化提炼。
items.translatedText: As AI models get better at handling tools, and as context windows get bigger without as much rot, you can start to design agents more similar to how people work instead of having to mitigate the model limitations with weird hacks. For instance, even a year ago, if you were to build an agent to process large amounts of documents, the state of the art was to do embeddings on the data, then do a similarity search and pull out the chunks of content that matched (as well as surrounding chunks). This was necessary because context windows could only accurately handle a small amount of information at a time. This worked surprisingly well given the constraints (at least assuming you were working with authoritative data only), but had a lot of tricky limitations because it’s not how humans work. For instance, what do you do if the chunks you sent to the model were the most relevant semantically, but actually rendered irrelevant by some other part of the document. For instance, if at the top of the document it says “do not use this” but on page 3 there is information that’s relevant, that data will be sent to the model as the top hit. Similarly, chunked data is difficult when you need various parts of a document or many documents to be understood for answering a problem. Today, increasingly, you can begin to have agents effectively use tools and work with information far more similar to how people work. This unlocks a qualitatively different set of use-cases and capability level that agents can now handle. As we were designing the Box Agent, these improvements allowed us to rethink our entire architecture for AI. The agent can now search data similar to how a user searches, but with the benefit of being able to expand their queries, do semantic search, and process results nearly instantly. Then the agent can either read many documents at a time or at least much larger amounts of context. Again, much more similar to people, but now at hyperspeed. Importantly, beyond tool calling and context windows, the reasoning of models has also gone up enormously. This means the agent can also know when it needs to search for information again when it didn’t find something it was looking for or if something feels off. As model progress continues on the dimensions of context accuracy, tool calling, advanced reasoning, and coding, agents are going to become insanely powerful.
items.originalText: As AI models get better at handling tools, and as context windows get bigger without as much rot, you can start to design agents more similar to how people work instead of having to mitigate the model limitations with weird hacks. For instance, even a year ago, if you were to build an agent to process large amounts of documents, the state of the art was to do embeddings on the data, then do a similarity search and pull out the chunks of content that matched (as well as surrounding chunks). This was necessary because context windows could only accurately handle a small amount of information at a time. This worked surprisingly well given the constraints (at least assuming you were working with authoritative data only), but had a lot of tricky limitations because it’s not how humans work. For instance, what do you do if the chunks you sent to the model were the most relevant semantically, but actually rendered irrelevant by some other part of the document. For instance, if at the top of the document it says “do not use this” but on page 3 there is information that’s relevant, that data will be sent to the model as the top hit. Similarly, chunked data is difficult when you need various parts of a document or many documents to be understood for answering a problem. Today, increasingly, you can begin to have agents effectively use tools and work with information far more similar to how people work. This unlocks a qualitatively different set of use-cases and capability level that agents can now handle. As we were designing the Box Agent, these improvements allowed us to rethink our entire architecture for AI. The agent can now search data similar to how a user searches, but with the benefit of being able to expand their queries, do semantic search, and process results nearly instantly. Then the agent can either read many documents at a time or at least much larger amounts of context. Again, much more similar to people, but now at hyperspeed. Importantly, beyond tool calling and context windows, the reasoning of models has also gone up enormously. This means the agent can also know when it needs to search for information again when it didn’t find something it was looking for or if something feels off. As model progress continues on the dimensions of context accuracy, tool calling, advanced reasoning, and coding, agents are going to become insanely powerful.

items.summary: Andrej Karpathy 提到：Wow, this tweet went very viral! I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The idea of the idea file is that in this era of LLM agents, there is less of a point/need of sharing…
items.whyItMatters: 这条内容保留了原始表达，但尚未完成高质量结构化提炼。
items.translatedText: Wow, this tweet went very viral! I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The idea of the idea file is that in this era of LLM agents, there is less of a point/need of sharing the specific code/app, you just share the idea, then the other person's agent customizes & builds it for your specific needs. So here's the idea in a gist format: You can give this to your agent and it can build you your own LLM wiki and guide you on how to use it etc. It's intentionally kept a little bit abstract/vague because there are so many directions to take this in. And ofc, people can adjust the idea or contribute their own in the Discussion which is cool.
items.originalText: Wow, this tweet went very viral! I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The idea of the idea file is that in this era of LLM agents, there is less of a point/need of sharing the specific code/app, you just share the idea, then the other person's agent customizes & builds it for your specific needs. So here's the idea in a gist format: You can give this to your agent and it can build you your own LLM wiki and guide you on how to use it etc. It's intentionally kept a little bit abstract/vague because there are so many directions to take this in. And ofc, people can adjust the idea or contribute their own in the Discussion which is cool.

items.author: Zara Zhang
items.role: Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: YouTube:
items.title: 用 OpenClaw 实现「脑泄」式待办管理
items.summary: 作者把待办事项直接通过消息发给 OpenClaw，OpenClaw 会自动执行任务，每天早上会推送任务完成报告，并标注需要关注的事项。
items.translatedText: 刚刚和 OpenClaw 有了一个顿悟时刻。我用「向 OpenClaw 脑泄待办」来替代我的待办清单。每当我想起一个快速任务，我就直接消息发给 OpenClaw。它不仅会记录这些任务，还会实际去执行这些任务。每天早上它会给我发送一份报告，告诉我哪些任务已经完成了，还会标注哪些需要我的关注。这可能真的会是一个有效的待办管理系统。
items.originalText: Just had an aha moment with OpenClaw. I'm replacing my to-do list with "braindumping to-dos to OpenClaw". Whenever I think of a quick task, I just message it to OpenClaw Not only will it record those tasks, but it will actually DO those tasks Every morning it sends me a report of what tasks are already done, and highlights the ones that need my attention This might actually be a to-do management system that works

items.author: Nan Yu
items.role: head of product @linear
items.title: Linear Agent 上新功能：可直接读代码回答产品问题
items.summary: Linear Agent 现在能直接读取代码，PM、销售或支持人员无需再频繁打扰工程师，就能查到产品具体配置等细节信息。
items.translatedText: 如果你是一名 PM 或从事销售或支持工作，你有多少次需要去打扰工程师才能弄清楚应用到底是怎么运作的？我当时想准确地知道我们所有用户的个人配置的默认设置是什么。现在你再也不需要为了这类事情去问工程师了，因为 Linear Agent 可以直接读取代码并告诉你。
items.originalText: If you're a PM or on sales or support, how many times have you needed to bother an engineer to find out exactly how the app works? I wanted to know exactly what the default setting was for a personal configuration for all of our users. You never need to ask an engineer again for this kind of thing, because Linear Agent can just read the code and tell you.

items.title: 推出Follow builders技能帮助追踪AI资讯
items.summary: 作者介绍了自己创建的Follow builders技能，精选25个来自OpenAI、Anthropic、Google等公司的AI builder账号和播客，可通过agent整合发送个性化每日通讯，GitHub上已获2000+ stars。
items.whyItMatters: 为AI从业者提供了一个经过筛选的高质量信息源聚合方案，解决了信息过载和筛选成本高的问题。
items.translatedText: 推出"Follow builders"技能：这是掌握AI领域惊人动态的最佳方式。我精心挑选了25个X账号和播客，它们分享来自OpenAI、Anthropic、Google、OpenClaw、Replit、Vercel、Cursor等公司builder的最高质量、第一手AI见解。你的OpenClaw/agent可以整合我的核心信息源，并根据你的偏好通过任意渠道发送个性化每日通讯。该工具已在GitHub上广泛使用，获得2000+ stars；链接如下。
items.originalText: Introducing the "Follow builders" skill: the best way to stay on top of the insane happenings in AI I carefully curated 25 X accounts & podcasts that share the highest-quality, first-hand insights on AI (by builders from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, OpenClaw, Replit, Vercel, Cursor...) Your OpenClaw/agent can remix my central feed & send you a personalized daily newsletter in whatever channel you like Already widely used with 2k+ stars on GitHub; link below

items.author: Dan Shipper
items.role: ceo @every   the only subscription you need to stay at the edge of AI
items.title: Linear的agent-native转型经验
items.summary: Dan Shipper认为SaaS并未消亡，只是需要成为agent-native。他以Linear为例说明：Linear没有盲目跟风推出chatbot，而是坚持帮助团队开发软件的核心使命，将用户群体从人类扩展到agents，使Coinbase、Brex等公司能够在Linear中管理agents的工作。
items.whyItMatters: 这条内容提供了SaaS如何成功转型的具体案例。Linear的经验表明：不追逐热点、坚守产品使命、让agents成为一级用户，是应对AI时代的有效策略。对其他SaaS公司有参考价值。
items.translatedText: SaaS并未消亡，只是需要成为agent-native。Linear是一个很好的例子：他们将产品 pivot 成人类和agents都能使用的方式，这使他们成为agent-native时代最顶尖的软件工具之一。
items.originalText: SaaS isn’t dead, it just needs to become agent-native. Linear (@linear) is a great example of how: They pivoted the product to be used by both humans and agents, and that has made them one of the premier software tools in the agent-native era. I had Linear’s cofounder and CEO @karrisaarinen on @every's AI & I to talk about how a product management tool for human software developers became an agent-native tool—and how Linear’s trajectory reveals a bright future for SaaS businesses: - Speed means decisions matter more, not less. AI makes it easy to have an idea and build it without considering whether its existence is justified. When ChatGPT was released, SaaS companies were launching their own chatbots left, right, and center. Instead of jumping on the bandwagon, Linear stopped to consider whether the application was useful. (It wasn’t.) - Just because the technology has changed doesn’t mean your mission should. Karri attributes Linear’s success to never losing sight of what matters: helping teams develop great software. Instead of chasing trends, Linear focused on understanding how AI was impacting its customers’ workflows—and updating its product accordingly. - Agents are now first-class users. Linear never tried to change what it was or did well; it just expanded the user base. Companies can now kick off agents inside Linear, manage them, and track what they're working on alongside the humans on the team, which explains why Codex, Coinbase, and Brex all run their agents on Linear. This is a must watch for anyone interested in how an agent-native SaaS company operates. Watch below! Timestamps: Introduction and how Every first discovered Linear: 00:00:39 Why Linear waited to ship AI features instead of rushing to chatbots: 00:02:00 Linear's agent platform and becoming the system that guides AI agents: 00:05:06 Why "SaaS is dead" is a simplistic narrative: 00:07:42 How Linear adopted AI coding tools internally: 00:12:18 AI's impact on product building workflows—speed versus thoughtfulness: 00:17:45 The value of conceptual work and thinking before shipping: 00:22:18 How AI is reshaping Linear's product strategy: 00:29:30 Demo: Linear's agent skills, shared context, and code review workflow: 00:37:18 The future of product development and the enduring role of human judgment: 00:47:48

items.author: Dan Shipper
items.role: ceo @every   the only subscription you need to stay at the edge of AI
items.title: Notion 适合 AI 时代
items.summary: Dan Shipper 表示从 2018 年接触 Notion 2.0 以来一直是粉丝和高级用户，认为 Notion 天然适配 AI 时代，与 Notion 合作很愉快。
items.whyItMatters: 作为 AI 产品公司 CEO，Dan Shipper 对 Notion 的认可可能影响其他 AI 产品团队对笔记工具的选择，Notion 在 AI 工具链中的定位获得开发者社区的积极反馈。
items.translatedText: 技术上这次活动是 Notion 赞助的，但从 2018 年我遇到 Ivan Zhao 他给我看了 Notion 2.0 以来，我一直是一个粉丝和高级用户。说实话，我觉得 Notion 就是为 AI 这个时刻而生的——很荣幸能和他们合作。
items.originalText: @every @NotionHQ technically this camp is sponsored by Notion but ive been a fan and power user since i met @ivanhzhao in 2018 and he showed me Notion 2.0 tbh i feel like notion was made for this moment in AI—a pleasure for us to work with them
